title: Optimal hedge ratio in a biased forward market under liquidity constraints
summary_en: Classical hedging theory assumes a frictionless forward market and unlimited funding. Real firms face both a bias in the forward curve (hedge is costly on average) and a hard funding limit on the margin they can post. This paper derives the optimal hedge ratio for a risk-averse firm under both frictions together. The answer departs substantially from the textbook minimum-variance hedge: the liquidity constraint makes the firm hedge less as the bias grows, because expected funding-cost losses can outweigh variance reduction. Gives treasurers a sharper decision rule than one-factor models.
data_used: Theoretical / analytical paper. Calibration examples use standard foreign-exchange forward parameters for EUR/HUF-type markets.
policy_relevance: Relevant to Hungarian corporate treasury practice and indirectly to MNB supervision of large corporate FX exposures. Supports a more conservative view of hedging incentives for liquidity-constrained firms than the standard one-period variance-minimising rule. Caveat: single-period, one-asset; extension to multi-period dynamic hedging would be the natural next step.
policy_instruments: MNB supervisory treatment of FX exposures
